text:
malicious remote operation of the breakers was conducted by multiple external humans using either existing remote administration tools at the operating system level or remote industrial control system (ICS) client software via virtual private network (VPN) connections. Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to log into a service that accepts remote connections, such as telnet, SSH, and VNC. The adversary may then perform actions as the logged-on user.

The companies believe that the actors acquired legitimate credentials prior to the cyber-attack to facilitate remote access. Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining Persistence.

The KillDisk malware erases selected files on target systems and corrupts the master boot record, rendering systems inoperable. Adversaries may wipe or corrupt raw disk data on specific systems or in large numbers in a network to interrupt availability to system and network resources.

It was further reported that in at least one instance, Windows-based human-machine interfaces (HMIs) embedded in remote terminal units were also overwritten with KillDisk. Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems, services, and network resources.

The actors also rendered Serial-to-Ethernet devices at substations inoperable by corrupting their firmware. Adversaries may overwrite or corrupt the flash memory contents of system BIOS or other firmware in devices attached to a system in order to render them inoperable or unable to boot, thus denying the availability to use the devices and/or the system.

the actors reportedly scheduled disconnects for server Uninterruptable Power Supplies (UPS) via the UPS remote management interface. Adversaries may shutdown/reboot systems to interrupt access to, or aid in the destruction of, those systems.

The malware was reportedly delivered via spear phishing emails with malicious Microsoft Office attachments. Adversaries may send spearphishing emails with a malicious attachment in an attempt to gain access to victim systems.

It is suspected that BlackEnergy may have been used as an initial access vector to acquire legitimate credentials; Adversaries may obtain and abuse credentials of existing accounts as a means of gaining Persistence.

text:
SandWorm threat group members primarily used spearphishing emails to gain access to computers or account credentials.
SandWorm heavily leveraged PowerShell commands and scripts to discover system information, execute code, and download malware.
Many of the spearphishing emails sent by SandWorm contained malware-laced documents that required user execution to deploy.
To maintain their foothold, SandWorm obtained and repeatedly used existing accounts' credentials to preserve persistence in victim systems.
SandWorm leveraged malware to escalate system privileges and determine whether particular antivirus processors were running, then attempted to identify other computers on the same network to potentially compromise.
SandWorm used an algorithm to obscure particular features of the Olympic Destroyer malware to obstruct post-attack investigations and avoid detection.
The group also attempted to obfuscate their activity by deleting data from compromised machines and servers.
On multiple occasions, SandWorm attempted to masquerade their activity through researching and emulating malware used by the Lazarus Group.
SandWorm dumped credentials to obtain account login and credential details from compromised machines.
SandWorm leveraged customized malware to overwrite itself to incorporate any additional usernames and passwords that it could obtain from the previous computer before spreading to the next computer.
SandWorm repeatedly accessed and browsed files, ran malicious scripts, and searched compromised machines for credential files.
SandWorm exploited remote services to gain unauthorized access to internal systems.
Once they gained access to the remote system, they deployed malware that was leveraged to obtain system privileges, extract and execute an open-source credential harvesting tool, and move laterally throughout the network.
After gaining access to victims' computers, SandWorm threat actors performed various functions designed to identify, collect, package, and view targeted data, including usernames, IP addresses, and server data relating to RDP sessions on the target computers.
This activity included stealing data.
SandWorm established command and control to create a single point of access between compromised networks and a server they controlled.
The tunnel allowed them to hide their activity, issue commands, install additional tools, and transfer data.
SandWorm leveraged legitimate credentials to exfiltrate data from a victim network and retrieve internal documents from machines inside victim environments.
SandWorm defaced approximately 1,500 websites and disrupted service to some of those websites following the Georgian web hosting provider's compromise.
The group deployed destructive malware to delete files from the hard drive, force shutdowns, and impede rebooting and recovery by misconfiguring BitLocker, rendering computers inoperable.

The destructive nature of this malware aims to render the machine unusable by deleting shadow copies, event logs and trying to use PsExec & WMI to further move through the environment. Adversaries may delete or modify artifacts generated within systems to remove evidence of their presence or hinder defenses.

Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

Adversaries may abuse Windows Management Instrumentation (WMI) to execute malicious commands and payloads.

a binary that, when executed, drops multiple Gles on to the victim host Adversaries may rely on users to execute a binary that initiates the infection process, such as dropping multiple files onto the victim host.

These Gles are embedded as resources (obfuscated). Adversaries may obfuscate files or information to hide malicious code or artifacts, making it more difficult for security tools to detect them.

These Gles are named using randomly generated Gle names Adversaries may use randomly generated file names to disguise the true nature of malicious files, making detection more difficult.

By checking the ARP table with the Windows API GetIPNetTable Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of services running on remote hosts and local network infrastructure devices, including those that may be vulnerable to remote software exploitation.

By WMI (using WQL) with the following request: "SELECT ds_cn FROM ds_computer". This request attempts to list all the systems within the current environment/directory. Adversaries may attempt to get a listing of other systems by IP address, hostname, or other logical identifier on a network that may be used for Lateral Movement from the current system.

execute it via a VBScript Adversaries may abuse Visual Basic (VB) for execution.

The malware dynamically updates this list after using the password stealers. Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

parses the registry and it queries the sqlite Gle in order to retrieve stored credentials Adversaries may interact with the Windows Registry to gather information about the system, configuration, and installed software.

Adversaries may attempt to dump credentials to obtain account login and credential material, normally in the form of a hash or a clear text password.

The stealer attempts to obtain credentials from LSASS Adversaries may attempt to access credential material stored in the process memory of the Local Security Authority Subsystem Service (LSASS). After a user logs on, the system generates and stores a variety of credential materials in LSASS process memory.

By leveraging cmd.exe from the host the malware Grst deletes all possible shadow copies on the system using vssadmin Adversaries may delete or remove built-in data and turn off services designed to aid in the recovery of a corrupted system to prevent recovery.

This step is executed to ensure that Gle recovery is not trivial Adversaries may delete or remove built-in data and turn off services designed to aid in the recovery of a corrupted system to prevent recovery.

The next step the attacker takes in this destructive path is to, again leverage cmd.exe, but this time use bcdedit, a tool used for boot conGg data information, to ensure that the Adversaries may delete or remove built-in data and turn off services designed to aid in the recovery of a corrupted system to prevent recovery.

to further cover their tracks the deletion of the System & Security windows event log is performed Adversaries may clear Windows Event Logs to hide the activity of an intrusion.

Additionally, the destroyer disables all the services on the system: Adversaries may stop or disable services on a system to render those services unavailable to legitimate users.

Additionally, the malware lists mapped Gle shares and for each share, it will wipe the writable Gles (using either uninitialized data or 0x00 depending of the Gle size). Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems, services, and network resources.

the destroyer shutdowns the compromised system Adversaries may shutdown/reboot systems to interrupt access to, or aid in the destruction of, those systems.

Additionally, the Olympic Destroyer drops the legitimate, digitally signed, PsExec Gle in order to perform lateral movement by using this legitimate tool from Microsoft. Adversaries may use Valid Accounts to log into a service that accepts remote connections, such as telnet, SSH, and VNC. The adversary may then perform actions as the logged-on user.

text:
wiping of Windows systems through the KillDisk malware Adversaries may wipe or corrupt raw disk data on specific systems or in large numbers in a network to interrupt availability to system and network resources.

destruction of serial-to-Ethernet devices through malicious firmware updates Adversaries may overwrite or corrupt the flash memory contents of system BIOS or other firmware in devices attached to a system in order to render them inoperable or unable to boot, thus denying the availability to use the devices and/or the system.

BLACKENERGY

CRASHOVERRIDE

After authentication opens HTTP channel to external command and control server (C2) through internal proxy Adversaries may communicate using application layer protocols associated with web traffic to avoid detection/network filtering by blending in with existing traffic.

Overwrites an existing service to point to the backdoor so the malware persists between reboots Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.

On execution, the malware attempts to contact a hard-coded proxy address located within the local network. Adversaries may use a connection proxy to direct network traffic between systems or act as an intermediary for network communications to a command and control server to avoid direct connections to their infrastructure.

The backdoor then sends a series of HTTP POST requests with the victim's Windows GUID (a unique identifier set with every Windows installation) in the HTTP body. This information authenticates the targeted machine to the command and control (C2) server.

If the authentication is successful to the internal proxy, the malware attempts to perform an HTTP CONNECT to an external C2 server via the internal proxy.

Create a new process as logged in user •Create a new process as specified user via CreateProcessWithLogon Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads.

Write a file •Copy a file •Execute a command as logged in user •Execute a command as specified user •Kill the backdoor Adversaries may abuse command and script interpreters to execute commands, scripts, or binaries.

Stop a service •Specify a user (log in as user) and stop a service •Specify a user (log in as user) and start a service •Alter an existing service to point to specified process and change to start at boot Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.

The service manipulation process is the only persistence mechanism for the malware. When used, the adversary can select an arbitrary system service, direct it to refer to CRASHOVERRIDE, and ensure it is loaded on system boot.

Loads payload modules which manipulate the ICS and cause destruction via the wiper Adversaries may execute malicious payloads via loading shared modules.

Starts itself as a service likely to hide better Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.

Launches the payload and begins either 1 or 2 hours countdown before launching the data wiper Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems, services, and network resources.

the ICS payload modules and data wiper module must be loaded by a separate loader EXE.

the sample analyzed starts a service named  defragsvc Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads as part of persistence.

It then loads the module DLL via an exported function named Crash Adversaries may execute malicious payloads via loading shared modules.

Control then passes from the launcher to the loaded module while the launcher waits two hours before executing the data wiper.

Clears all registry keys associated with system services •Overwrites all ICS configuration files across the hard drives and all mapped network drives specifically targeting ABB PCM600 configuration files in this sample •Overwrites generic Windows files Adversaries may destroy data and files on specific systems or in large numbers on a network to interrupt availability to systems, services, and network resources.

Renders the system unusable Adversaries may stop or disable services on a system to render those services unavailable to legitimate users.

Once executed, the data wiper module clears registry keys, erase files, and kill processes running on the system.

The first task of the wiper writes zeros into all of the registry keys in: SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services

'Kills' legitimate the master process on the victim host Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads.

Masquerades as the new master Adversaries may attempt to manipulate features of their artifacts to make them appear legitimate or benign to users and/or security tools.

the first action is to try to kill the communications service process which acts as the master process Adversaries may create or modify system-level processes to repeatedly execute malicious payloads.

In some circumstances, it may have no immediate impact while in others it could put customers into an outage. It is important to note that grid operations encompass failure modes and operations can normally compensate.

The outcome of the action infers that various systems can either perform actions on wrong information or report incorrect information to system operators. This Denial of Visibility will amplify misunderstanding and confusion while system operators troubleshoot the problem as their system view will show breakers closed when they are open. Adversaries may manipulate physical process control within the industrial environment. Methods of manipulating control can include changes to set point values, tags, or other parameters. Adversaries may manipulate control systems devices or possibly leverage their own, to communicate with and command physical control processes. The duration of manipulation may be temporary or longer sustained, depending on operator detection.

A second, and more severe, amplifying attack would be to neutralize the automated protective system by creating a Denial of Service against some or all of the protective relays. Adversaries may perform Denial-of-Service (DoS) attacks to disrupt expected device functionality.
